metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the registry caller adapts a legacy `record` dict into `(pae_bytes, signatures)` for `HmacScheme.verify`: specify that `pae_bytes = signing._canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest)` (reconstructed from record fields) and `signatures[0].sig = record['signature']`, OR redesign `HmacScheme.verify` to accept the legacy `record: dict` and `ticket_id: str` directly to match how `signing.verify_record` is actually called.
findings.finding: The HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) dispatch contract uses a DSSE-style interface (pre-serialised bytes + a signatures list with .sig objects) that does not match the shape the legacy HMAC path actually needs: signing.compute_signature requires ticket_id and manifest as separate arguments to reconstruct _canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest), and legacy records carry 'signature' as a flat hex string — not as signatures[0].sig. The plan leaves unspecified (a) how the registry caller produces pae_bytes for the HMAC scheme (who calls _canonical_payload, and with what ticket_id/manifest?), and (b) how signatures[0].sig is derived from a legacy record dict. An implementer following the plan verbatim will build an adapter whose input shapes don't match what signing.py actually consumes.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section — HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dispatch contract, replace the abstract pae_bytes/signatures description with a concrete one: HmacScheme.verify receives the legacy record dict + ticket_id (matching signing.verify_record's actual call signature), derives the expected hex by calling signing.compute_signature(ticket_id, record['manifest'], trust_root), and compares it to record.get('signature','') via hmac.compare_digest. This keeps the adapter faithful to the existing path with no reimplementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the dispatch contract whether `pae_bytes` passed to `HmacScheme.verify` is the raw canonical JSON payload (matching `_canonical_payload`) or true DSSE-PAE; if the latter, describe the bridging logic that reconstructs `_canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest)` from the PAE-encoded envelope for the HMAC computation.
findings.finding: The plan's `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)` dispatch contract asserts it 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over `pae_bytes`', but the legacy HMAC in `signing.py` computes over `_canonical_payload()` (canonical JSON bytes of `{v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest}`), not DSSE Pre-Authentication Encoding (PAE) bytes. No PAE implementation exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section: 'HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Describe in the dispatch contract (or in the `HmacScheme.verify` spec) how a legacy HMAC record's flat `signature` hex string is adapted into the `signatures` list the Scheme protocol expects, or confirm that the caller constructs this adapter before invoking `HmacScheme.verify`.
findings.finding: The dispatch contract asserts `HmacScheme.verify` constant-time-compares to `signatures[0].sig`, but existing legacy HMAC records store the signature as `record["signature"]` (a plain hex string), not a `signatures` array. No `signatures` array structure exists in any legacy record or in `signing.py`.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section: 'constant-time-compares (hmac.compare_digest) to signatures[0].sig'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `HmacScheme.verify` reconstructs the legacy `_canonical_payload` (JSON-canonical) bytes rather than DSSE PAE bytes, since all stored HMAC signatures were produced over JSON-canonical payload — or explicitly mark the registry dispatch path for HMAC as non-PAE and explain how legacy records are routed to `signing.verify_record` instead.
findings.finding: `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, ...)` is specified to compute HMAC-SHA256 over DSSE PAE bytes (`pae_bytes`) and compare to `signatures[0].sig`, but ALL existing stored HMAC signatures were computed over `_canonical_payload` (sorted-key compact JSON via `canonical_bytes`), not PAE bytes. Routing a legacy record through the new `HmacScheme` via the registry dispatch would always produce `mismatch`, contradicting AC2 (test matrix) and AC3/AC4 (regression: existing attestations must return `certified` via the new path).
findings.location: Dispatch contract section / AC2 / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the dispatch contract, replace 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes' with 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over the legacy canonical JSON payload (via signing._canonical_payload / signing.compute_signature), NOT over PAE bytes — HmacScheme is an intentional anomaly that wraps the existing signing.py logic verbatim so existing records verify correctly.' Adjust the `verify` signature to accept the manifest and ticket_id needed to reconstruct the canonical payload, or simply delegate to `signing.verify_record`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `signatures` parameter type (element schema: dataclass with `.sig` attribute, or dict, or other) and specify the fallback verdict when `signatures` is empty or `signatures[0].sig` is absent/falsy.
findings.finding: The dispatch contract references `signatures[0].sig` to extract the stored HMAC value, but never defines what `signatures` is — its type, element shape, and what `.sig` means (an attribute of a dataclass? a dict key?). Critically, the fallback when `signatures` is an empty list (`len(signatures) == 0`) is not specified — an empty list would raise `IndexError` rather than fail closed.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section — `HmacScheme.verify` body
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the dispatch contract: '`signatures` is a `list[Signature]` where `Signature.sig: str`; when `len(signatures) == 0` or `signatures[0].sig` is falsy, return `{"verified": False, "verdict": "mismatch", ...}` (treat missing sig as non-match, not key error).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define explicitly whether `HmacScheme.verify` returns a plain `dict` (matching `signing.verify_record`'s existing contract) or a `Verdict` typed object, and confirm this matches the `Scheme` protocol return type from task 518b.
findings.finding: `Verdict(verified=False, verdict="foreign_key")` is used as a constructor in the dispatch contract, implying a named `Verdict` type, but no such class/dataclass exists anywhere in `src/rebar` — `signing.verify_record` (the authoritative path) returns a plain `dict`. The plan never defines whether `Verdict` is a dataclass, TypedDict, namedtuple, or plain dict, leaving the implementer to guess and risk a type mismatch with every existing consumer of `verify_record` results.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section — `HmacScheme.verify` return type
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `Verdict(verified=False, verdict="foreign_key")` with `{"verified": False, "verdict": "foreign_key"}` (plain dict) to match `signing.verify_record`'s established return shape, and state 'same dict shape as `verify_record`' in the dispatch contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit verdict for an empty `signatures` list (e.g., `Verdict(verified=False, verdict="no_signature")`) in the dispatch contract, and add it as a case in the AC2 test matrix.
findings.finding: The dispatch contract specifies `signatures[0].sig` to extract the HMAC signature but does not specify what `HmacScheme.verify` should return when `signatures` is an empty list — this would raise an `IndexError` rather than failing closed, and the AC2 test matrix ('mismatch on wrong key, foreign_key on empty key') does not include an empty-signatures scenario.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section ('HmacScheme.verify … computes HMAC-SHA256 … compares to signatures[0].sig') and Acceptance Criteria AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the dispatch contract: 'if `signatures` is empty or `signatures[0].sig` is absent, return `Verdict(verified=False, verdict="no_signature")` (fail closed).' Add a corresponding row to the AC2 test matrix: 'empty signatures list → no_signature'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `register_legacy_schemes()` is idempotent (e.g., skip registration if the scheme id is already present in the registry), and add an AC or test asserting that calling it twice raises no error.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `register_legacy_schemes()` is idempotent (safe to call multiple times). If the underlying registry raises on duplicate key registration, test suites that trigger multiple imports or call the function directly will fail non-deterministically; if it silently overwrites, duplicate registrations could mask later asymmetric-scheme registrations.
findings.location: `src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py` — `register_legacy_schemes()` bootstrap; `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` — call site
findings.suggested_fix: In the `register_legacy_schemes()` implementation, guard each `registry.register_scheme` and `POLICY` assignment with a 'if not already registered' check (or use `setdefault`-style semantics), and document this contract in the plan's dispatch contract section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that task 518b is in `done` status and `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` with its `Scheme` protocol, `get_scheme`, `resolve`, `register_scheme`, and `POLICY` symbols is committed before this task is started; add an explicit task dependency.
findings.finding: The plan's prerequisite assertion that `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` exists (from task 518b) is unverifiable: the `src/rebar/attest/` directory does not exist in the repo, and task 518b is still `in_progress`. All symbols the plan references from that module (`Scheme` protocol, `get_scheme`, `resolve`, `register_scheme`, `POLICY`) are absent from the codebase.
findings.location: Context section: 'new attest scheme registry (src/rebar/attest/registry.py, task 518b)'; Files section: `src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py`; AC lines 1-4
findings.suggested_fix: Add `518b-0558-fd18-4713` to this task's `deps` field so the workflow enforces ordering, and confirm the Scheme protocol signature matches what this plan's `HmacScheme.verify` implements.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit `deps` on tickets 9fd5 (DSSE + `attest/__init__.py` creation) and 518b (registry + `registry.py` creation) to enforce correct landing order, since this ticket modifies `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` created by 9fd5 and imports symbols from `registry.py` created by 518b.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` is created by sibling ticket 9fd5 (confirmed in its `file_impact`) and is also modified by this ticket to call `register_legacy_schemes()` at import. The plan does not enumerate the ordering dependency on 9fd5 (or 518b), and the current ticket's `deps` list is empty. If 9fd5 or 518b have not yet landed when this ticket is implemented, `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py` will not exist for this ticket to append to.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/attest/__init__.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge AC3 and AC4 into a single regression AC that explicitly names `signing.sign_manifest`/`signing.verify_signature`, specifies that kinds `plan-review` and `completion-verifier` are tested, and asserts `verdict == 'certified'` — eliminating the near-duplicate.
findings.finding: AC3 and AC4 are near-duplicate regression criteria: both require a test that signs and verifies a manifest via signing.py and asserts `certified`. AC3 specifies `sign_manifest`/`verify_signature` with kind context; AC4 repeats the same scenario generically. Having two separate ACs for the same observable behavior creates redundant test work and obscures whether they measure distinct things.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 and AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC3+AC4 with: '[ ] Regression: calling `signing.sign_manifest` then `signing.verify_signature` on a `plan-review` and a `completion-verifier` attestation still returns `verdict == "certified"` — the `signing.py` source file has no diff (verified by test fixture that imports and exercises the unchanged functions).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design note (ADR entry, CLAUDE.md section, or inline module docstring in `hmac_legacy.py`) that documents: (1) the import-time bootstrap pattern and why it is used, (2) how POLICY entries are expected to be superseded during the op-cert asymmetric cutover, and (3) the naming/lifecycle convention for future `register_*_schemes()` functions.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel 'import-time bootstrap registration' pattern (`register_legacy_schemes()` called in `__init__.py`) and hardcoded POLICY entries for legacy kinds that are explicitly expected to evolve (HMAC→asymmetric cutover is a named future epic), but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc captures the lifecycle contract for how/when these POLICY entries are superseded or how the bootstrap pattern should be followed for future scheme registrations.
findings.location: src/rebar/attest/__init__.py + src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py, T5e(c) documentation and T5e(b) changeability
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py` that explains the legacy registration lifecycle and points to the op-cert epic as the cutover mechanism; update CLAUDE.md with a one-paragraph note on the bootstrap registration pattern for scheme authors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether HmacScheme.verify is intentionally forward-only (for future DSSE-wrapped HMAC records only) and, if so, update the plan's stated purpose and AC#2 to reflect that; OR specify how HmacScheme.verify bridges the PAE-input vs JSON-signed-payload gap for existing legacy records (e.g. by reconstructing the canonical payload from the envelope body rather than using raw pae_bytes).
findings.finding: HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, ...) cannot verify any existing legacy HMAC record, because legacy records were signed over _canonical_payload JSON bytes (not DSSE-PAE bytes), yet the plan's stated purpose is 'so the substrate acknowledges HMAC and existing attestations keep verifying unchanged.' The regression test (AC#3/AC#4) explicitly sidesteps HmacScheme.verify and delegates to signing.verify_signature — meaning the new scheme wrapper serves a hypothetical future DSSE-wrapped-HMAC path, not the current done-definition of verifying existing records. This makes the pae_bytes interface a premature abstraction: YAGNI, with no current call-site that can supply PAE-encoded bytes for a legacy HMAC record.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section / AC#2 ('HmacScheme.verify over DSSE-PAE bytes returns certified')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale for the hmac_legacy.py shim approach (e.g. 'a separate shim keeps the security-critical signing.py untouched and isolates the registry-wiring concerns from the canonical HMAC implementation, which is already battle-tested and must not be disturbed during this expand phase').
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why a new hmac_legacy.py shim file (rather than, e.g., implementing the Scheme protocol directly on signing.py's existing verify_record logic) is the right boundary choice for this expand phase.
findings.location: Files section / Context section — no rationale statement present

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `HmacScheme.verify` recovers the original canonical-JSON signed payload from `pae_bytes` when verifying legacy HMAC records (or document that `HmacScheme.verify` bypasses PAE and applies HMAC directly to `_canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest)` recomputed from structured fields), and add an AC that asserts `registry.verify('plan-review', legacy_envelope, key)` returns `certified` for a record signed by `signing.sign_manifest` — not only that the untouched `signing.verify_signature` path works.
findings.finding: The Dispatch contract specifies `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, ...)` computes HMAC-SHA256 over `pae_bytes`, but existing legacy HMAC signatures were created by `signing.compute_signature` over `canonical_bytes({v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest})` — ordinary JSON bytes, not DSSE-PAE-encoded bytes. A PAE-encoded input will produce a completely different HMAC digest and will never match any stored legacy signature. The plan asserts the HmacScheme can bridge this gap (AC2: 'certified with correct key', AC3/AC4: regression guarantee) without specifying how the canonical-JSON payload is derived inside `HmacScheme.verify` when the input is PAE bytes.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section: 'HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes'; AC2 (verify returns certified with correct key); AC3/AC4 (regression: existing attestations still certify via signing.verify_signature).
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dispatch contract, clarify that `HmacScheme.verify` does NOT apply HMAC to raw `pae_bytes`. Instead, it unpacks the PAE payload to extract `(ticket_id, manifest)`, recomputes `canonical_bytes({v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest})`, and applies HMAC-SHA256 to that canonical payload — matching exactly what `signing.compute_signature` stored. Add an explicit AC: `registry.verify('plan-review', envelope_for_legacy_record, key) == Verdict(verified=True, verdict='certified')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the dispatch contract how `HmacScheme.verify` bridges the DSSE-style `pae_bytes` interface to the legacy `_canonical_payload` JSON bytes that stored HMAC signatures were actually computed over (e.g. by stating that for the HMAC legacy scheme `pae_bytes` IS the `_canonical_payload` output, or that `HmacScheme` extracts `ticket_id`+`manifest` from context and calls `compute_signature` directly).
findings.finding: The dispatch contract specifies `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, ...)` 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over `pae_bytes`', but all existing legacy HMAC records were signed over `signing._canonical_payload()` bytes — compact sorted-key JSON of `{v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest}` — not DSSE Pre-Authentication Encoding bytes. These two byte sequences are structurally different. The plan never specifies how `pae_bytes` is translated to (or equated with) the `_canonical_payload` JSON bytes that the stored `signature` field was actually produced from, leaving a processing protocol gap that would make every legacy record verify as `mismatch`.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section — `HmacScheme.verify` body / Files section — `src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py`

findings.finding: 'Legacy records' is undefined throughout the plan: the dispatch logic (AC1) must know what distinguishes a legacy record from a non-legacy one, but no definition is given (e.g., absence of a 'scheme' field, scheme field equals null, scheme field equals the HMAC algorithm id, or something else). An executor cannot implement the dispatch branch without this decision.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: 'verify dispatches to it for legacy records'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'A record is considered legacy when it lacks a top-level `scheme` field (or `scheme` is null); the dispatcher defaults to the HMAC signer in that case.'

findings.finding: The plan names a 'registry' and a 'dispatch' mechanism (AC1: 'HMAC signer is registered as a scheme keyed by its algorithm id; verify dispatches to it') but no corresponding module, class, or function exists anywhere in the codebase — this is a new artifact whose concrete location (file path, class name, public API) is never specified in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; Context section

findings.finding: AC1 states 'verify dispatches to it for legacy records' but never specifies the dispatch contract: which field on the stored record triggers legacy-HMAC lookup (e.g. `record['algorithm'] == 'HMAC-SHA256'`, absence of a `scheme` field, or something else), leaving the boundary between legacy and future dispatch paths undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Dispatch on `record.get('algorithm', 'HMAC-SHA256') == 'HMAC-SHA256'` so both old records (no field) and current records (field present) route to the legacy scheme.

findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'scheme registry' to register HMAC into already exists ('registered as a scheme keyed by its algorithm id; verify dispatches to it for legacy records'), but no such registry or algorithm-keyed dispatch infrastructure exists anywhere in `signing.py` or the broader codebase. `verify_record()` calls `compute_signature()` directly with no algorithm routing table, and no `scheme_registry`, `get_scheme`, or algorithm-dispatch structure was found by Grep.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The HMAC signer is registered as a scheme keyed by its algorithm id; verify dispatches to it for legacy records.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Creates' section to the plan listing the new scheme-registry abstraction (e.g. `_SCHEME_REGISTRY: dict[str, ...]` in `signing.py`) and update AC 1 to say 'Creates a scheme registry keyed by algorithm id and registers HMAC-SHA256 as the first (legacy) scheme; `verify_record` dispatches through it.'

findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any of the three acceptance criteria. AC1 ('dispatch routes to HMAC for legacy records'), AC2 ('regression test: prior attestation returns verdict `certified`'), and AC3 ('no behavior change') each say what should be true but provide no test filename, test command, or fixture description that would produce evidence of success.
findings.location: All three Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Example additions: AC1: `pytest tests/test_registry.py::test_legacy_hmac_dispatch -v`; AC2: add fixture `tests/fixtures/legacy_attestation.json` (a real pre-change attestation) and run `pytest tests/test_signing.py::test_legacy_attestation_still_certified -v`; AC3: `pytest tests/test_signing.py -v` (existing suite must stay green).

findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the new registry dispatch handles records where the `algorithm` field is absent or None. Currently `verify_record()` ignores the stored algorithm entirely and always applies HMAC-SHA256; under a dispatch-by-algorithm-id design, a missing/null key has no defined fallback, which would silently change behaviour for any hand-crafted or pre-field legacy record.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC1: 'verify dispatches to it for legacy records'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit note: 'When `record["algorithm"]` is absent or None, the registry dispatch MUST default to the HMAC-SHA256 scheme (mirroring the current unconditional path) so no existing record changes verdict.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify WHERE the registry lives (new module? existing signing.py? a separate registry.py?), WHO performs the registration, or WHEN it executes (at module import time, via an explicit init call, or via a decorator), leaving the CREATE phase of the shared-state lifecycle undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 / Context section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note such as: 'Registration is performed at module-import time in `src/rebar/signing_registry.py` via a `register()` call in the module body; consumers import this module before calling `verify`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
